content:
```json
[
  {
    "id": "q1",
    "question": "In similarity-based learning, what is the core assumption that allows a model to make predictions on new data?",
    "options": ["Similar inputs have similar outputs.", "The data must be linearly separable.", "All features are equally important.", "The model must have low variance."],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "The foundational principle of similarity-based learning is that if two data points are close to each other in the feature space (i.e., they are similar), they are likely to have the same label or output value."
  },
  {
    "id": "q2",
    "question": "Which of the following algorithms is a classic example of a similarity-based learning method?",
    "options": ["k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)", "Support Vector Machine (SVM)", "Decision Tree", "Logistic Regression"],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "The k-Nearest Neighbors algorithm is a quintessential example of similarity-based (or instance-based) learning. It makes predictions for a new instance by finding the 'k' training examples that are most similar to it."
  },
  {
    "id": "q3",
    "question": "What is a significant computational drawback of basic, brute-force k-NN during prediction time?",
    "options": ["It requires calculating the distance to every point in the training set.", "It is prone to overfitting on small datasets.", "It cannot handle categorical features.", "It requires a lengthy training phase to build a model."],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "Since k-NN is a lazy learner (it does no work during training), all computation is deferred to prediction time. A brute-force approach requires comparing the new instance to every single instance in the training dataset, which becomes computationally expensive for large datasets."
  },
  {
    "id": "q4",
    "question": "When using k-NN, why is it crucial to normalize or standardize the features?",
    "options": ["To prevent features with larger scales from dominating the distance calculation.", "To ensure the data follows a Gaussian distribution.", "To reduce the number of dimensions in the dataset.", "To convert all features into categorical values."],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": Features exist on different scales (e.g., age 0-100 vs. salary 30,000-100,000). Without normalization, the distance metric would be disproportionately influenced by the feature with the largest scale, making the others less relevant.
  },
  {
    "id": "q5",
    "question": "In the context of k-NN, the 'curse of dimensionality' refers to which specific problem?",
    "options": ["In high-dimensional spaces, the concept of 'nearest neighbors' becomes meaningless because all points are roughly equidistant.", "It becomes impossible to visualize the data beyond three dimensions.", "The model's training time increases exponentially with each new feature.", "The risk of overfitting decreases as more features are added."],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "As the number of dimensions grows, the volume of the space increases so fast that the available data becomes sparse. This sparsity makes distance measures less effective, as the contrast between the nearest and farthest neighbor shrinks, making the concept of a 'nearest' neighbor less meaningful and harming predictive performance."
  }
]
```
